# Incident Learning Standard

**Flexibility: Expected (SHOULD)**

## The Standard

Every significant incident SHOULD produce a structured learning document
within a defined timeframe. The purpose of the document is organizational
learning, not individual accountability. Incidents are treated as signals
about system behavior, not evidence of personal failure.

An incident is "significant" when it causes user-visible impact, triggers
emergency response, reveals a previously unknown failure mode, or when
the team judges that the near-miss was close enough to warrant analysis.
The threshold should err toward inclusion — an incident that produces no
learning document is a missed opportunity, not a saved cost.

## What Good Looks Like

- Every significant incident produces a learning document within a
  defined window (typically 5-10 business days after resolution).
- The learning document follows a consistent structure: timeline,
  impact, contributing factors, what went well, what could improve,
  and concrete action items with owners and due dates.
- Blameless culture is practiced, not just proclaimed. Documents focus
  on system conditions that made failure likely, not on who made the
  mistake. Language is reviewed to remove finger-pointing.
- Action items are tracked to completion in the team's normal work
  system — they do not live only in the learning document.
- Patterns are analyzed across incidents. Recurring contributing factors
  are identified and addressed structurally, not just per-incident.
- Learning documents are discoverable by anyone in the organization.
  Institutional memory grows from each incident rather than resetting.
- Review meetings are held to discuss findings. The meeting focuses on
  understanding, not on assigning blame or rubber-stamping the document.

## Gap Analysis Guide

- **No learning documents produced.** Incidents are resolved and
  immediately forgotten. The same failure modes recur because the
  organization has no memory.
- **Documents exist but are blame-oriented.** Root cause analysis
  terminates at "human error" rather than examining the system
  conditions. People learn to hide incidents rather than report them.
- **Action items are not tracked.** The document lists improvements
  but nobody follows up. The same recommendations appear in multiple
  documents.
- **Documents are not discoverable.** Learning stays within the
  responding team. Other teams encounter the same failure modes
  independently.
- **No pattern analysis.** Each incident is treated as unique. Systemic
  issues that manifest across incidents are never identified.
- **Threshold is too high.** Only catastrophic incidents produce
  documents. Near-misses, which outnumber actual failures, are ignored.

## 1970/2070 Note

Aviation adopted structured incident learning in the mid-20th century
and proved its value across decades. The discipline predates software
and will outlast current technology stacks. Whether the incident involves
a mechanical failure in 1970 or an autonomous system misalignment in
2070, the practice of extracting organizational learning from failure
remains essential. The tools change; the discipline endures.

## Baseline+Overlay Note

This eng-domain standard extends JOS's universal governance. It operates
in addition to, not instead of, meta-layer standards.

## Connected Objects

- JOS-P21 — the principle this standard implements
- JOS-C23 — the concept from which this standard derives

### Framework Connection

Framework §15 (Incident as a HAPPENS-axis work type) and §10 (divergence detection) enrich incident learning — incidents are formally classified as work types, and the divergence model identifies cases where compliance masks poor outcomes, feeding richer learning.
